title: Burst Balloons
problem_statement: You are given n balloons, indexed from 0 to n - 1. Each balloon is painted with a number on it represented by an array nums. You are asked to burst all the balloons. If you burst the ith balloon, you will get nums[left] * nums[i] * nums[right] coins. Here left and right are adjacent indices of i. After the burst, the left and right then becomes adjacent. Find the maximum coins you can collect by bursting the balloons wisely. Note: You may assume nums[-1] = nums[n] = 1.
intuition: The order matters because bursting a balloon affects the multipliers for its neighbors. We need to consider subarrays and choose the optimal balloon to burst last in each subarray.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: Try bursting each balloon first, recurse on the remaining array. For each burst, add coins = left * current * right.
approaches.why_not: Factorial time due to trying all permutations of bursting order. Infeasible for n>10.
interview_commentary: This approach tries every possible order of bursting. For each subarray, choose which balloon to burst last, add the coins, and recurse on left and right subarrays.
The time is factorial because there are n! orders, and for each, we do O(n) work.
This is correct but too slow for n=500.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Same recursion, but memoize on (left, right) to store max coins for subarrays.
approaches.why_not: Still O(n^3), but bottom-up is the same complexity and iterative.
interview_commentary: To optimize, I'll memoize on the subarray (left, right).
Each subarray is computed once, and for each, we try O(n) choices for the last burst, so time O(n^3).
Space O(n^2) for the memo table.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Interval DP
approaches.description: Fill dp[l][r] for increasing lengths. For each length, for each l, for each k in [l,r], dp[l][r] = max over k of dp[l][k-1] + coins + dp[k+1][r].
approaches.why_not: This is the optimal solution for this problem.
interview_commentary: Bottom-up: process subarrays by increasing length. For each length, for each start l, for each possible last burst k in [l, r], update dp[l][r].
This ensures when we compute dp[l][r], the smaller subarrays are already filled.
Time O(n^3) because for each of O(n^2) subarrays, we try O(n) k's.

interview_qa.a: Greedy might burst the highest value first, but that could leave low multipliers for neighbors, reducing total coins.
dp[l][r] = max coins for bursting balloons from l to r, with l-1 and r+1 as neighbors.
Because the problem states nums[-1] = nums[n] = 1, so they act as multipliers.
O(n^3) is acceptable for n=500, as 500^3 = 1.25e8 operations.
